title: Mass spectrometric characterization of polyhedral oligosilsesquioxanes and heterosilsesquioxanes
abstract:
Mass Spectrometric Characterization of
Polyhedral Oligosilsesquioxanes and
Heterosilsesquioxanes
Ray Bakhtiar1* and Frank J. Feher2*
1Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Stevens Institute of Technology, Castle Point on Hudson, Hoboken, NJ
07030, USA
2Department of Chemistry, University of California, Irvine, CA 92717, USA
Silsesquioxanes constitute a diverse range of Si-O frameworks with a wide variety of intriguing possibilities
as models for catalysis. These topologically interesting molecules are formed by the hydrolytic condensation
of trifunctional organosilanes. Heterosilsesquioxanes are formally derived by substitution of a main-group,
transition-metal, or f-element atom for one or more Si atoms in a silsesquioxane. A number of
silsesquioxanes and their metal-containing derivatives, metallasilsesquioxanes (Si-O-M), were character-
ized using atmospheric pressure chemical ionization (APCI) and turbo ion-spray (TISP) mass spectrometry.
The use of n-hexane or n-hexane/ethanol (90:10, v/v) allowed the techniques to be utilized to characterize a
large number of complexes. In some cases addition of trace amounts of ammonium acetate, sodium chloride,
or sodium acetate to the ethanolic portion of the solvent allowed the detection of [M  NH4] or [M  Na]
ions. Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 20 January 1999; Revised 12 February 1999; Accepted 14 February 1999
The term ‘silsesquioxane’ refers to structures with an even
number of silicon atoms (i.e. [RSiO3/2]n with n = even
integer) and extended network solids (i.e. [RSiO3/2]) which
can achieve the idealized empirical formula for silsesquiox-
anes, (RSiO3/2)?. In order to avoid confusion, it is often
useful to distinguish between ‘fully condensed’ and
‘incompletely condensed’ silisesquioxane frameworks. A
fully condensed silsesquioxane has an empirical formula of
[RSiO3/2]n, whereas an incompletely condensed silses-
quioxane possesses reactive Si-OH groups. A host of
heterosilsesquioxanes
including main-group, transition-
metal
and
lanthanide
metallasilsesquioxanes
are
also
available via functionalization of incompletely condensed
silsesquioxanes. Recent applications include soluble models
for silica-supported catalysts, and precursors to hybrid
inorganic/organic materials.1–14 For these reasons metalla-
silsesquioxanes generated from the coordination of incom-
pletely condensed silsesquioxanes to high-valent transition
metals have emerged as useful solution-state models for
silica-supported catalysts.15 A wide range of silsesquiox-
anes can be prepared via the hydrolytic condensation
reactions of trifunctional organosilicon monomers, as
presented in Scheme 1.
Herein, using mass spectrometry, we present evidence on
the generation and characterization of a selected group of
silsesquioxanes in neat hexane or n-hexane/ethanol (i.e.
90:10, v/v). The results in this study indicate that atmo-
spheric pressure chemical ionization (APCI) and turbo ion-
spray (TISP) mass spectrometry (MS) could be useful
Scheme 1.
*Correspondence to: R. Bakhtiar, Department of Chemistry and
Chemical Biology, Stevens Institute of Technology, Castle Point on
Hudson, Hoboken, NJ 07030, USA.
Contract/grant sponsor: National Science Foundation.
Contract/grant sponsor: Phillips Laboratory.
CCC 0951–4198/99/080687–08 $17.50
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
RAPID COMMUNICATIONS IN MASS SPECTROMETRY
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
 analytical methods for the rapid characterization of low
molecular weight silsesquioxanes and their metal-contain-
ing derivatives (metallasilsesquioxanes). The two techni-
ques yielded comparable spectra and, therefore, the results
of the TISP-MS will be the focus of this article.
EXPERIMENTAL
High purity solvents were purchased from Fisher Scientific
(Springfield, New Jersey, USA) and all other chemicals
were
synthesized
and
purified
using
published
pro-
cedures.1–11,15 An API III Perkin Elmer-Sciex (Thornhill,
Ontario, Canada) triple quadrupole mass spectrometer was
used. A description of the instrumentation has been
documented elsewhere.16–24 The mass spectrometer was
coupled to a Shimadzu LC-10AD pump and an SCL-10A
system controller (Shimadzu, Columbia, Maryland, USA).
Calibration was performed on both the first quadrupole
(Q1) and the third quadrupole (Q3) using a solution of
polypropylene glycol (PPG) in 3 mM ammonium acetate.
The orifice (OR) potential was maintained at 65 Volts (V) in
the positive ion detection mode. For APCI operation, the
inlet probe was maintained at 420°C, which allowed liquid
to be nebulized and vaporized (at a flow of 1.0 mL/min with
no split) into the corona discharge region of the APCI
source. High purity nitrogen was used to assist nebulization.
The auxiliary gas (high purity nitrogen) flow rate was 3 L/
min.
The
discharge
needle
current
was
set
at
4.8
microamperes.
Typically a stock solution of 0.01 mg/mL of silsesquiox-
ane was freshly prepared. The high performance liquid
chromatography (HPLC) system was used to deliver
0.95 mL/min of mobile phase composed of n-hexane or n-
hexane/ethanol (i.e. 90:10, v/v) to a T-connector. A syringe
pump (Model 22, Harvard Apparatus, South Natick, MA,
USA) was utilized to deliver the silsesquioxane solution at a
flow rate of 50 mL/min to the T-connector. Subsequently, a
net flow rate of 1.0 mL/min of solvent was introduced into
the APCI source. Caution: Hexane is a flammable organic
solvent. In order to minimize the possibility of an ignition,
nitrogen was used as both the auxiliary and nebulizing gas.
This nitrogen flow assisted in displacement of oxygen from
the APCI source compartment to prevent ignition of
nebulized hexane. Additional safety measurements regard-
ing the use of hexane in conjunction with HPLC/MS are
described elsewhere.25,26
All experiments were repeated using a turbo ion-spray
(TISP) interface. In this interface, a stream of heated dry
nitrogen is directed at the droplets produced by ion-spray. In
contrast to the APCI-MS experiments, lower flow rates
yielded satisfactory results. For example, a direct flow rate
of 20 mL/min was typical using a probe temperature of about
300°C, auxiliary flow of 4 L/min, nebulizer pressure of 50
psi and spray voltage of 4800 V. Other instrumental
parameters included scan step = 0.25 Da, dwell time = 5
msec, and number of averaged scans = 5–10.
CS ChemDraw Pro 3.5 (Cambridge Software Corpora-
tion, Cambridge, MA, USA) and Isotope Pattern Calculator
(Les J. Arnold, University of Waikato, Hamilton, New
Zealand) were utilized to calculate molecular masses and
isotopic distributions, respectively.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Recently, Hong et al.27 reported the characterization of
highly charged dendrimers with encapsulated cuboid
octasilsesquioxanes using electrospray ionization mass
spectrometry (ESI-MS). Polyhedral silsesquioxanes are
used in the fabrication of ceramic materials and liquid
crystalline supramolecular systems. These dendrimers are
soluble in chloroform and dichloromethane and partially
soluble in tetrahydrofuran and acetone. ESI-MS and P-31
{1H} (proton spin decoupling) NMR spectroscopy were
used to study the homogeneity of these charged macro-
molecules with molecular masses up to 5500 Da. Hong et
al.27 demonstrated that the deposition of monodendrons on
the core cuboid center was complete with no major
imperfection. Devreese et al.28 reported the application of
matrix-assisted laser desorption time-of-flight mass spectro-
metry for the analysis of a bis(silsesquioxane)-tungsten
Figure 1. Molecular structures of silsesquioxanes discussed in the text.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
688
MS OF OLIGO- AND HETEROSILSEQUIOXANES

Figure 1. Molecular structures of silsesquioxanes discussed in the text.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
MS OF OLIGO- AND HETEROSILSEQUIOXANES
complex. In addition, we recently presented the first
example of APCI mass spectrometric characterization of
neutral silsesquioxanes in neat hexane.29 As an extension of
our previous contribution, we initiated our studies with a
survey of the APCI- and TISP-MS of a number of
silsesquioxanes and heterosilsesquioxanes. One of the
limitations that is faced in APCI- or TISP-MS of these
classes of complexes is their solubility. Thus, the selection
of solvent is critical for utilizing these ionization methods.
According to our observations, APCI- and TISP-MS
afforded similar spectra with the advantage that the latter
could be operated at lower flow rates (i.e. 20 mL/min).
Therefore, we will focus on the results obtained by TISP-
MS throughout this paper. The chemical structures of the
silsesquioxanes and heterosilsesquioxanes which were
examined using MS are shown in Figs 1 and 2, respectively.
Figure 2. Molecular structures of metallasilsesquioxanes discussed in the text.
Figure 3. The positive ion mode TISP spectra ([M  H] and [M  Na]) for complex 1.
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
MS OF OLIGO- AND HETEROSILSEQUIOXANES
The isotopic contribution of silicon (28Si, 29Si and 30Si at
percent relative abundance of 92.23, 4.67 and 3.10,
was observed in all the mass spectra.
Similarly, the isotopic distributions of metallasilsesquiox-
anes were confirmed by comparison with the simulated
Figures 3–6 illustrate the representative TISP mass
spectra for several silsesquioxanes. According to Figs 3
and 4, complexes 1 and 2 readily added adventitious
quantities of Na and their spectra showed dominant peaks
corresponding to [M  Na] ions. The simulated isotopic
patterns for both species are shown in the bar mode. The use
of added alkali metal salts as an ionization reagent has been
discussed previously.30–33 Complexes 3 and 4 yielded
simple spectra with dominant peaks corresponding to their
[M  H] ions. In fact, the TISP mass spectrum obtained for
Figure 4. The positive ion mode TISP mass spectrum (profile mode, [M  Na]) versus theoretical isotope pattern
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
MS OF OLIGO- AND HETEROSILSEQUIOXANES
Figure 5. The positive ion mode TISP mass spectrum obtained for complex 6. As shown, a dominant signal at m/z 1585 was
observed for [M  Na] ions. The inset displays the observed isotopic pattern. The signal at m/z 1600 corresponds to
Figure 6. The positive ion mode TISP mass spectrum obtained for complex 8. As shown, a dominant signal at m/z 1266 was
observed for [M  NH4] ions. The inset displays the observed isotopic pattern. Presumably a variation in the OR potential
did not significantly alter the spectra (panel A: OR = 65 V; panel B: OR = 160V).
Copyright # 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 13, 687–694 (1999)
MS OF OLIGO- AND HETEROSILSEQUIOXANES
4 was identical to its APCI mass spectrum which was
recently reported.29 In contrast, species 5 yielded a
predominant signal corresponding to [M  Na] ions.
Figures 5 and 6 display the spectra obtained for complexes
6 and 8. When dissolved in n-hexane/ethanol (90:10, v/v),
complex 7 afforded a spectrum dominated by its [M  H]
ions. TISP-MS of complex 6 resulted in a relatively simple
and clean spectrum at higher orifice voltage (OR = 145 V)
with predominant peaks corresponding to its [M  Na] and
[M  K] ions (Fig. 5). Likewise, complex 8 yielded a
dominant signal corresponding to its [M  NH4] ions (Fig.
6). Complex 9, a chloride salt, was dissolved in n-hexane/
ethanol/methanol (80:10:10, v/v/v) and its spectrum ex-
hibited a [M  H] signal at m/z 881.
Satisfactory TISP mass spectra were acquired for all the
metallasilsesquioxanes (Fig. 2). Complexes 10 and 12 were
dissolved in n-hexane/ethanol (80:20, v/v) and yielded base
peaks corresponding to their [M  H] and [M  Na] ions
at m/z 1093, 1115, 1038 and 1060, respectively. The partial
dimerization of complex 12 was reported to be enthalpically
favored (DH° = ÿ6.02  0.27 kcal/mol) with a large nega-
tive entropy for dimerization of DS° = ÿ17.1  0.2 eu.3,4
We were unable to obtain conclusive data on the existence
of the dimer using APCI- or TISP-MS. It is conceivable that
the dimer of 12 dissociates during the droplet formation and
ion evaporation processes (i.e. jet of heated dry nitrogen at
300°C) within the mass spectrometer interface. A decrease
in the TISP temperature elicited a dramatic loss in mass
spectral signal. Likewise, under our experimental condi-
tions, complex 11 yielded a predominant signal correspond-
ing to its [M ÿ CH3  H] ions.
Figures 7 and 8 show the TISP mass spectra, obtained for
complexes 13 and 14, respectively. Complex 13 readily
fragmented to yield a base peak corresponding to its
[M ÿ N(CH3)2  H] ions (Fig. 7). The facile elimination
of the [N(CH3)2] moiety is not surprising and an analogous
observation, using ferrocene-derived phosphine ligands, has
been reported by Henderson and co-workers.32 TISP-MS of
complex 14 yielded a relatively simple spectrum which
[M  Na] ions (Fig. 8). Complex 15 was sprayed using a
solution of n-hexane/ethanol (75:25, v/v) and afforded a
weak signal corresponding to its [2M  H] ions. The
reaction of [(c-C6H11)7Si7O9(OH)3] with BI3 forms the
dimer of complex 15, which has been characterized by
NMR and single-crystal X-ray diffraction.1 As described by
Feher et al.,1 structure 15 adopts a non-bridged dimeric
structure with trigonal planar BO3 moieties which can
maximize p-bonding between oxygen and boron. Lastly,
TISP-MS of complex 16 generated signals at m/z 1629 and
1665 which were assigned to its [M-Cl  H] (base peak)
and [M  H] ions, respectively.
Although some fragmentation was detected (i.e. complexes
11 and 13), the above observations suggest that TISP-MS
can be a valuable tool in characterization of low molecular
weight silsesquioxanes and metallasilsesquioxanes. TISP
appeared to be rugged in allowing low flow rates and direct
introduction of the organic solvent yielding satisfactory
spectra. Molecular weight information was obtained using
